data.title: Inside AVSD: A Diversified ESG Play On International Equities
data.description:
The Avantis Responsible International Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:AVSD) brings together an unique approach to international investing. It combines ESG criteria along with factor based methods to arrive at a highly diversified portfolio with an emphasis on smaller market cap, higher profitability and value. The universe is all ex-US developed markets. Emerging markets are not in scope. The net expense ratio of 0.23% is reasonable for a rule based active ETF. The current yield is around ~3%, average levels expected from an international value fund.

Portfolio Characteristics

The international allocation follows expected lines with more than 20% allocation in Japan. UK is the next biggest exposure at ~13%. Unlike some international ETFs, Canada is in scope and has a significant ~11% weight. Inclusion of Canada is significant because of two reasons. One, the high correlation between US and Canadian markets are no higher than perceived. Choosing Japan over Canada, for instance, on correlation grounds, is not a long term diversification. There have been periods where Canada and Japan have diverged, usually only a few weeks to months (barring 2021-22). And for long term investors, the correlation almost always reverts. Inclusion of Canada, in fact brings in benefits of better potential growth avenues - something that has historically plagued Japan and Europe based international ETFs.

The sectoral allocation, as expected in international value plays is heavily tilted towards financials. Industrials and Consumer stocks are also weighted strongly. IT gets a low ~8% weight. Clearly the emphasis is on value and growth expectations should remain subdued. But that is how the international play usually looks like. IT as a growth lever is more valid for the US than in most of the ex-US developed world.

What sets AVSD apart is that it is not only heavily loaded in financials like its benchmark (MSCI World ex USA IMI), it is significantly overweight. It is also slightly overweight on consumer discretionary. Energy stocks are almost missing because of conflicts with the fund's ESG mandate. As a result of the ESG mandate, AVSD reports less than a third of the weighted average carbon intensity as the benchmark.

Concentration risk is extremely low by individual stock weights (although geographic concentration exist). Compensating any potential impact of ESG filters affecting returns negatively, the portfolio size is more than 2000. No single stock can impact overall returns disproportionately (top 10 contributing only ~7.5%). The top 10 names also carry known banking names like the Commonwealth Bank of Australia, HSBC and the Royal Bank of Canada. Other value names like Novartis show the value focus of the ETF.

The average market cap of ~$55b shows that it is a heavily large cap tilted fund, not the small cap emphasis its methodology expects. The value factors are expected to overpower small caps eventually, leaning towards larger caps. So the diversification by market caps gets diluted.

Performance

Comparison of AVSD against a ESG themed ETF like VSGX shows how well the factor tilts are working in favor of AVSD, that too not a strong market for value plays. Against another similar themed ETF based on ESG screen and factor methodology, DFSI, the performance turns out quite similar with minor outperformance in the past few months.

To see how the ESG layer is impacting returns, I compared AVSD against its non ESG equivalent, AVDE. While the ESG screen has not led to significant return gains in the short tenure so far, it has more importantly, not led to drag in the ETF's performance either. I was expecting that given AVSD's high diversification. Riding an ESG themed ETF now appears not a hit on performance.

Finally, the most important comparison is with the popular IEFA ETF, which is not constrained by ESG screens and is one of the benchmarks when it comes to international ETFs. AVSD doesn't do any significantly worse than IEFA overall, and there are periods where there is significant outperformance (for instance the post tariff sell off bounce back).

Is AVSD a Buy?

On its own territory, AVSD passes most comparison tests. For an international ETF, AVSD is at par with the most popular and most invested names (such as IEFA). For investors with an ESG mandate, it could be a decent buy, as performance comparisons show reasonable alpha versus peers.

However, I rate AVSD as a Hold. Passive investing in the international markets looks a little outdated over the past decade or more. Particularly if the ETF is focused on developed markets. The last real outperformance from the segment came in the lost decade. I usually advise a DIY styled international portfolio comprising of US listed international equities and regional ETFs. And a good mix of emerging markets. That makes the appeal of international equity meaningful if I were to look outside the US.

The hedging or diversification benefits are also limited. Even in the recent tariff sell-off, AVSD held up quite well compared to the US markets, but eventually fell with minor differences to a similar value focused SPYV that is US based. Lack of correlations coming to the rescue during US specific downturns is a thing of the past (at least in a longer investment horizon) with most US large caps like Microsoft and Cocoa Cola having in built international diversification. Any drawdown hedges that AVSD can provide, comes more from its value tilt. Investors may therefore even try US value ETFs as a hedge in addition to regional international ETFs with less focus on Europe.

If a broad international ETF has to be considered, I recommend lesser known names such as CGDG, IPKW and IDVO with a better growth tilt while providing risk diversification benefits. AVSD looks promising for an ESG version of international diversification, but as seen ESG screens do not drag performance significantly. There is no reason therefore that there cannot an ESG themed international ETF leveraging from the structural benefits of IPKW, CGDG and IDVO. Till such a proposition comes across, I will keep AVSD on hold, unless ESG is a mandate.

data.title: Burberry: The Market Is Already Discounting The Brand's Recovery
data.description:
The Q1 FY2026 report has just been released and Burberry (OTCPK:BURBY) is already up 3% despite the stock having already recorded a huge triple-digit surge since April 8.

Since Josh Schulman became CEO last year, the stock seems poised to regain lost ground. Of course, this is not happening without sacrifices, as the company is implementing a radical cost-cutting process that includes laying off 1/5 of its workforce.

Q1 FY2026 shows another decline in revenue, but much less severe than last year. As for profits, there are no indications: H1 and H2 provide much more information in this regard.

Highlights Q1 FY2026

Despite having a global brand that is appreciated worldwide and triple-digit growth in its share price, Burberry is still a struggling business. The positive market reaction is due to the fact that its future looks less dire than expected, but it remains complex nonetheless.

Retail revenue for this quarter was £433 million, down 6% compared to the same quarter last year. Adding fuel to the fire was an unfavorable exchange rate that impacted retail revenue by 4%; otherwise, the decline would have been only 2%.

As for comparable store sales, a key metric for understanding how attractive the brand is, analysts had expected a 3% decline, but it was only 1%. The market viewed this news as positive, but I would like to remind you that we are still talking about a decline: Burberry's problems are far from being resolved.

About half of its revenue comes from Asia, 30% of which is from China, and this region is still going through a challenging period.

It is encouraging to see that the EMEIA and Americas regions have returned to growth (+1% and +4% respectively), but Greater China and Asia Pacific are down 5% and 4%.

Asia remains the root of the problems for the entire luxury sector. For years, it drove the growth of high fashion companies to the point of becoming the most important region, and it has not been possible to offset its contraction with other geographical areas. Perhaps Burberry's mistake was to lean too heavily towards sales in Asia.

Now Burberry is paying the price and undergoing a transformation that should lead it to become a more balanced company. The slump in sales in Asia is gradually increasing the weight of other geographical areas, and a cost-cutting process is also underway.

Annual cost savings of £80 million are expected in FY2026, of which £24 million were already achieved in the previous fiscal year. The corporate restructuring, known as ‘Burberry Forward’, will cost £50 million this year and aims to achieve a variety of objectives:

  • Investments in products where there is a competitive advantage, such as scarves and coats.
  • Improving inventory efficiency and the way products are presented in stores and online.

Overall, to make the Burberry brand perceived as a symbol of ‘timeless British fashion’. In recent years, the company has diversified its product offering but has not realized that it has strayed too far from what made it great. It needs to return to its roots, minimizing waste, and this has been Josh Schulman's goal since he took the helm at Burberry.

Over the past year, we have moved from stabilising the business to driving Burberry Forward with confidence. The improvement in our first quarter comparable sales, strength in our core categories, and uptick in brand desirability gives us conviction in the path ahead. Our Autumn 2025 collection is being well received by a broad range of luxury customers as it arrives in stores. Although the external environment remains challenging and we are still in the early stages of our transformation, we are encouraged by the initial progress we are starting to see.

Joshua Schulman, Chief Executive Officer, Q1 FY2026 comment

La strada per la ripresa è ancora lunga, ma la collezione Autunno 2025 promette bene.

Conclusion

Compared to last year, the decline in sales has made huge progress, although there has not yet been overall growth. In FY2025, Burberry experienced double-digit declines in all geographical areas, with China close to -30%. That is why the market has rewarded it so much in recent months.

The recovery is gradual, and with hindsight, the new CEO's decision to eliminate the dividend last year is proving to be the right one. With the money saved, the company is financing the ‘Burberry Forward’ project, which could finally stabilize its profitability.

However, as competent as I believe the CEO to be, he cannot work miracles. Half of the company's revenue depends on Asia, and especially China, and we know that the Chinese are not currently inclined to spend on luxury goods. The real estate crisis has hit the country hard, and the Chinese central bank is easing monetary policy in order to stimulate consumer spending. These are macroeconomic issues that are beyond Burberry's control.

Personally, I find the CEO to be a very capable manager who is willing to swallow bitter but necessary pills (such as cutting dividends). This is the right approach to emerge from a very complex situation, but beware of volatility in this company: if you make 100% in a few months, you can also lose a lot in a short time.

I believe that the recent rise in the share price has already discounted most of the benefits of Burberry Forward, which is why I no longer consider the company a buy.

Of course, if the company returns to its former glory, it could be traded again at $30 per share, but Burberry has suffered a severe blow in terms of profitability that should not be underestimated. Just because the brand is recognized worldwide does not mean that recovery will be immediate. It could take several years, if not longer.

After a very strong rally, I always tend to be quite cautious, but this could be one of my limits. After a 100% rise in a few months, but with fundamentals still in contraction, I would not be surprised to see some profit-taking in the coming weeks. After a 15-20% pullback, Burberry could become a buy again.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: JD.com Has An Involution Problem
data.description:
JD.Com (NASDAQ:JD) is an attractive stock. 9 out of the last 10 Seeking Alpha articles have rated it a "buy" or "strong buy" and only two contributors have rated it a "sell" in the last three years. It's hard to argue with the consensus, but its performance does not equate with the strong fundamentals.

At the current price of $32.69, it is trading at 2019 levels and even at prices traded in 2014, its first year on the Nasdaq.

This article looks at why such a good company has such a bad performance.

JD Overview

JD is, at its core, an online marketplace operating mostly in China. Its limited international reach sets it apart from its main competitors, Alibaba (BABA) and Pinduoduo (PDD), as does its ownership of inventory and logistics.

Q1 earnings were released in May and showed the company is in good shape. Revenues are growing in double digits.

Free cash flow was nearly $3B in Q1, and EPS is growing YoY, although a dip is expected this year.

Margins are fairly tight, which is a concern, but also something that can be improved on going forward.

Non-GAAP operating margin was 3.9% for the first quarter of 2025, compared to 3.4% for the first quarter of 2024. Operating margin of JD Retail before unallocated items was 4.9% for the first quarter of 2025, compared to 4.1% for the first quarter of 2024.

Infrastructure has expanded, and the company is gaining a foothold in the EU through Poland. Domestic activity has also expanded, and in March, "JDL officially launched its operations center in Hong Kong, marking a significant step-up in expanding the coverage of its express delivery network and boosting service efficiency in the region."

Increased efficiency and coverage could help improve margins.

The two things that really stand out are JD's strong finances and its low valuation. Its forward PE ratio is only 10.44, compared to BABA at 12.77 and PDD at 12.4. Amazon (AMZN) has a PE ratio of 35.9.

You have to wonder why it is so low. This isn't a case of being under the radar - just look at the coverage on Seeking Alpha. It relates to the extremely challenging deflationary environment.

The China Problem

The US trade war with China is a weight on the Chinese economy, which was already fragile. While tariffs have been reduced from the extremes of early April, they are 35% higher this year and could weigh on activity.

The tariffs will affect international exporters such as PDD more than JD, but a sluggish economy will not help domestic consumer spending, which is already weak and leading to deflation. CPI turned negative in mid-2023 and has slipped back into negative territory again in 2025.

ING included this commentary with the above chart:

One of the main reasons is a contractionary cycle featuring heavy price competition as well as pay freezes and pay cuts. This will be a difficult challenge to tackle. Policymakers, though, recently shifted attention toward addressing the problem, with an eye on improving market exit mechanisms, encouraging consolidation and restructuring, and addressing non-market practices resulting in excessive price competition.

"Excessive price competition" is bad news for JD, which is in a low-margin business with prices racing to the bottom. Even worse, it owns its inventory, which is losing value over time.

Involution

"Involution" is a term that has been around for years and generally describes intense competition within a group that leads to stagnation and burnout. It's generally a societal experience, but is also being used to describe business practices in China, where prices are aggressively discounted to the detriment of the companies involved. Policymakers in Beijing have started using the term more and are looking to address this unhealthy price competition.

At the Davos World Economic Forum in June, China's Premier Li Qiang pledged to make the country a "mega-sized consumption powerhouse." Details were scant, but the government and central bank have a variety of tools at their disposal to boost consumer demand. Rate cuts are an option, but due to the deflationary environment, yields have been on a path lower anyway, and a cut in the short end won't be a quick fix.

Li also said China's development was "not a short-term expansion but a steady progression toward long-term goals." It does seem they are playing the long game, and the (mostly) self-inflicted popping of the property bubble shows they can pain in the short-term.

Assuming the government does now address involution and tackle deflation, JD would be well placed for further growth and could demand a higher multiple. This is a solid company operating under a very challenging retail environment that may be about to change.

Outlook

The last major JD rally came in Q4 2024 when the government last announced stimulus. This move broke the downtrend, but soon ran out of steam and drifted lower again.

Earnings have been solid, but aren't having a sustained effect on price moves. JD seems to be hanging around for the next big announcement and we don't know what or when that will be. The risk is that price drifts lower, but it may be worth taking a position in advance of any news. $26-27 is where the price broke out of the downtrend last year and is a good starting point. Upside targets are initially at the $48 area and the "double top," but I do think JD can break this resistance and move higher into the $50s.

Risks

Beijing is playing the long game and seems unconcerned with short-term pain. Addressing involution could initially have a negative effect. Higher prices could suppress demand further.

JD has good finances so it should be able to withstand a further slump, but its stock price may return to the 2024 low of $19 where it has found support many times over the years. If that breaks, it would signal some deep problems.

Conclusions

JD is a profitable company with strong finances and an attractive valuation. However, the stock is drifting lower as the environment for retail in China is extremely challenging. Excessive price competition driven by soft demand is a major issue, but this "involution" is something policymakers in Beijing have recently vowed to address. Unfortunately, the government are playing the long game and there is no quick fix. I therefore continue to rate JD a "hold" as it could lack direction for some time. $26 seems an attractive buy-in point in anticipation of a better macro backdrop in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Gladstone Investment: A Fair Price For A Great Business
data.description:
Valuation has been a recurring theme of my coverage over the past several months. The valuation of assets across the market has been stretched to levels that I call priced to perfection. Looking at the S&P 500, the earnings yield is now considerably below the risk free rate. According to Multpl, the S&P is now trading at 29.9x earnings, or an earnings yield of 3.3%. Compared to a ten year treasury that yields nearly 4.5%, this looks like an extraordinary price to pay for the largest companies in the United States.

Looking elsewhere, we'll see that assets look similarly expensive. Credit spreads have compressed considerably since the depths of the tariff madness when bond prices began to decline. Today, spreads have tightened considerably, even in the high yield space. This means those who are searching for value are forced to begin looking elsewhere. The traditional big two asset classes look stretched. Equities are unlikely to surge again with their valuations baking in major earnings growth over the next several years. Bonds need rates to fall considerably to deliver the "equity-like" returns that investors seek from high performing fixed income. Two tantalizing catalysts that are keeping investors on their feet as the market continues to froth into 2025.

As it feels fair to stick to the theme of valuation, we are going to explore a sector off the beaten path. Looking beyond the large capitalization names carrying the market and bonds that offer modest income, we are going to seek returns in a highly valued market. Accordingly, we must get creative and begin looking where others are not. Enter stage - business development companies. Today's discussion will center around Gladstone Investment (NASDAQ:GAIN), a long-tenured business development company that maintains a reasonable valuation in a rich market. We will explore the company's price to book multiple and the recent acquisition of a major security infrastructure company.

Setting The Stage

Gasp! Even business development companies are looking expensive these days. Following the short lived panic caused by the tariff madness, the market has hit its stride and risk has gone out the window. As I just mentioned, equities and fixed income are both looking relatively expensive and we are venturing into the weeds to find some value. This means exploring other sectors that might be less familiar to investors, like business development companies or REITs. As is always the case, not all players are created equal.

In fact, I would argue that some of the top names in the industry are looking similarly overvalued in a frothy marketplace defined by sky high expectations for growth. Let's look at two specific examples.

First, let's examine one of the largest participants in the sector, Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC). In February, I published an article on ARCC discussing the company's Q4 earnings release and corresponding pricing.

To put it succinctly, ARCC has little room for upside, trading at a compressed yield and expanded price to book value. While there's no indication that the company is running into trouble, I believe there is little upside to be found by investing at current prices.

Today, ARCC continues to trade at 1.16x forward book value, around 10% higher than their historical average of 1.05x book value. Typically with ARCC, patiently waiting for an opportunity to purchase at or below book value has been favorable.

More recently, I just published an article on Main Street Capital (MAIN), one of the most well known brands in the sector.

Unfortunately, MAIN is priced exuberantly at around double its book value. Historically, this has set the company up for a sharp price decline, as occurred in March and April.

If shares of MAIN are to return to their historical mean valuation of around 1.6x book value, there is once again a 20% decline in our future. At the current price to book value to 2x, the multiple is around 25%-30% higher than historical averages. I would also argue that this comes during a time carrying more risk for BDCs, not less.

What if an investor wants an equity heavy BDC priced at a reasonable valuation? They should look no further than GAIN.

Review Of Company & Coverage

GAIN is a publicly traded business development company from the Gladstone fund family. The company manages other BDCs and REITs in niche sectors. Perhaps most notable is the company's farmland REIT, Gladstone Land (LAND). GAIN focuses on the acquisition, recapitalization, and ongoing management of mature companies in the middle market across several segments, consumer products, and business/consumer services. GAIN does not invest in startups, preferring to target mature, stable companies.

GAIN typically makes debt investments between $5 million and $30 million and equity investments between $10 million and $40 million. GAIN actively targets companies with revenue in the range of $20 million and $100 million and EBITDA from $4 million to $15 million. GAIN seeks minority ownership of targets and prefers to hold a board seat. GAIN's typical hold period is seven years with an exit via recapitalization, initial public offering, or sale to third party.

I have covered GAIN on a variety of occasions in the past. Most recently, I published an article on GAIN in 2024, opening up the company's business model and explaining why the company was positioned to perform well in a defrosting IPO landscape.

GAIN is one of few truly unique operators in the BDC space. While business development companies are typically a dime a dozen, GAIN is a private equity investor hiding as a public company. For shareholders, this means an opportunity to capitalize on private investments in a liquid, scalable, and transparent format.

GAIN continues to trade at a small premium to net asset value, currently 1.06x. However, this premium/discount is more complex than meets the eye. Given the large allocation to equity investments, GAIN's portfolio is likely worth more than current NAV would suggest. GAIN continues to be a 'buy' off of strong tailwinds and an impressive strategy that continues to deliver.

Since publication, GAIN has returned 24% to shareholders on a combination of share appreciation and distributions paid. After an active year of acquisitions and dispositions, GAIN has delivered to shareholders in more way than one. Below are GAIN's one year price and total return.

GAIN's emphasis on mature businesses has created stability and long term growth for GAIN. Let's explore this piece of the business further before diving into the valuation.

The Gladstone Difference

GAIN takes a largely differentiated approach to BDC investing. While most business development companies function mainly as lenders for companies, GAIN takes a different style. While the company still loans to portfolio buisnesses, their less-than-secret weapon is the company's equity investments in portfolio businesses. In their acquisitions and recapitlizations, GAIN also makes an equity investment and genreally seeks a board position.

GAIN specifies four primary characteristics they like to see in prospective acquisiton targets: Experienced Management, Value-Orientation/Positive Cash Flow, Strong Competitive Positioning, and Enterprise Collateral Value. This four spoke startegy helps identify companies that are mature and "boring" in terms of predictable growth and cash flow.

The company typically targets an equity investment totaling 25% of their portfolio. Below is an excerpt from the cmopany's most recent annual report.

We expect that our investment portfolio over time will consist of approximately 75% in debt investments and 25% in equity investments, at cost. As of March 31, 2025, our investment portfolio was comprised of 73.3% in debt investments and 26.7% in equity investments, at cost.

Combined with participating preferred and the company's equity positon rises to more than one third of the portfolio.

So, what does a typical deal look like for GAIN?

Let's explore the company's recent investment in Global Grab Technologies. Global Grab is a provider of security systems for infrastructure around the world. This includes perimeter security and vehicle mitigation (think surveillance, fences, and guard gates around airports, etc). GAIN's investment included a combined $67.6 million debt and equity package to the company.

While Global Grab's balance sheet is not readily available to investors, infrastructure security is a stable industry in a world where dynamic threats are growing and emerging.

These acquisitions are part and parcel to GAIN's business model. The acquisition and eventual disposition of a company like Global Grab lays the groundwork for the company's significant special distributions which have occurred for several years. Most recently, GAIN paid a special dividend of $0.54 per share in June.

[object HTMLElement]

The Valuation Is Appealing

So, having reviewed the company's business model, the question becomes what makes GAIN attractive relative to other BDCs. Well, let's explore…

From my chair, there are a limited number of BDCs that are like GAIN in terms of strategy or execution. The company's equity emphasis means many traditional BDCs are not necessarily all that comparable given they have some slightly different performance drivers. It is generally important to be looking apples to apples when making comparisons.

I see Main Street Capital (MAIN) as one of the most comparable business development companies to GAIN. Setting aside the differences in management structure (internal vs external) and balance sheet growth (GAIN is considerably smaller), there are significant overlaps.

Both companies target the middle market with an equity kicker to their investments. Both companies target old, established businesses with stable operations. Both have generated impressive returns on equity over long time periods. Looking over long time horizons, GAIN has been the superior pick to MAIN. GAIN has outperformed MAIN handily over the past decade, establishing a winning 10-year period.

More recently, MAIN has overtaken GAIN as a golden child in business development companies. However, we should look at the source of this outperformance. Let's cut the riff raff and get right to it. Over the past year, MAIN has outshined GAIN considerable due to an expanding valuation.

As of today, MAIN trades over 2x book value based on the company's recently released estimates. Despite non-accruals continuing to rise to some of the highest levels in the industry, MAIN shares are powering ahead with exuberance. This situation led me to publish an article yesterday explaining why MAIN could see another double digit decline in the near future.

Meanwhile, GAIN is trading at book value, priced just 5% above the company's estimated net asset value. While MAIN's performance has come largely on account of investor enthusiasm tending higher, GAIN has actually delivered to shareholders in distributable cash flow and NAV growth. Better yet, the company is actually trading below its historical mean valuation of 1.06x book value.

This leaves these two BDCs in a radically different position. GAIN is still firing on all cylinders and has plenty of room to run as management continues to remain active in redeploying the proceeds of portfolio exits. Trading in line with book value provides a fair price to enter this great business.

Outlook

Another important piece of the GAIN puzzle is the outlook around private equity. The company's equity heavy strategy means business valuation is as important as the ability of portfolio companies to repay their indebtedness to GAIN. Luckily for us, private equity is heating up after several years of weakness that have challenged the market. Let's explore deeper.

Ernst & Young recently published their private equity pulse, which provides a market overview of buyouts and mergers. In Q1, deal volume increased by 45% year over year, driving overall deal value to more than twice the comparable period from the prior year.

Even more specifically, EY sees opportunity emerging in the middle market, GAIN's backyard. The company says that opportunities are likely to emerge across the U.S. middle market with manufacturing businesses taking the lead.

Conclusion

The BDC segment is large and getting larger. As companies continue to emerge and join the growing industry, sniffing out quality amidst growing competition becomes increasingly important. As with REITs, not all businesses are created equal. Generally, I recommend shying away from the new kids on the block, showing favor to some of the industry's established competitors instead. This has worked out well with companies like GAIN, MAIN, and ARCC giving healthy returns to shareholders over long time frames. As the industry continues to perform well, GAIN earns a Buy.

data.title: DuPont: Neutral Rating Was Correct - Looking Post-Qnity
data.description:
I invest quite a bit in chemical companies, and world-leading ones are sort of at the top of my list in the sector. These include companies like BASF SE (OTCQX:BASFY), which are generally the leaders in larger parts of the business, but also specific dominating leaders like Brenntag (OTCPK:BNTGY), which focuses on distribution. However, it also involves leaders like DuPont de Nemours, Inc. (NYSE:DD), a company steeped in a strong history and tradition. I have shares in the business, but following challenges in the sector went neutral on the company and gave it a hold about 1.5 years ago. Since that time, I have not bought more, nor have I sold off the entirety of my position (I sold 50% in the fall last year at a decent profit, but unfortunately not all).

The company has returned 4.6% in over 1.5 years, which is not great, given that the S&P 500 (SP500, SPY) is up 32%+ in the meantime, giving us the following performance.

As you can see, the right choice would have been to take profits in October last year, which I should have done with more conviction. Now, the company has recovered from a short-term decline, and I mean to update my thesis for the future.

DD is not the highest-yielding chemical business, but I will make the argument that it is one of the safest, and one that could have an upside at this time.

Why?

Let's take a look.

Dupont - Update for 1Q25 performance and the upside in 2025-2027E

We have the latest set of results, which are now roughly 1.5 months old. Some changes from my 2024 thesis are warranted, given the changes that DD seems to be intent on making to its portfolio. It's no wonder the company is doing this, given a 200+ year history and a portfolio of chemicals that has evolved with time and demand.

The company's current plan is to spin off its electronics business as the next sort of step in its "growth/change phase." This business will be called Qnity, which will leave DuPont with some key technologies. This includes safety and protection like Kevlar (doubt they are ever giving that up), as well as Tyvek. Both of these technologies are legacy, and both are used in key segments such as textile and construction, generating over $8B in sales, with around 40% of a pro-forma amount of annual sales coming from legacy protection (protection includes Tyvek also).

The new pro forma DuPont will see a heavier correlation to housing, which is currently set to grow with nearly 1.5M starts in the next 10 years. The remaining 60% of the new revenue will come from specific end markets, including healthcare, water, and mobility, at about 20% each. You also know I love chemical companies that work with water - this is actually one of the reasons I pushed into DuPont in the first place.

On paper, this company will therefore serve a diverse set of end markets, with an (in my opinion) very appealing overall mix. The end markets include, but are not limited to, healthcare, EV, aerospace, construction, and machinery, with water especially being something I focus on, given the increased need for water filtration as well as water "generation" (from things like salt water and the like). I think we'll see more companies pushing into this.

Overall, I'm not negative on this change - I view it as potentially appealing, even though I have a bit harder time following the decision rationale. Strategic focus and unlocking value are mentioned, with increased agility for the company, as well as shareholder value.

I'm personally not convinced, but time will tell - I don't view it as something that will impact what remains in DuPont negatively, however.

That being said, I'm not that interested in investing in Qnity - the reason I invested in DuPont was not the electronics sector, generating a pro-forma standalone $4.3B, but the other businesses. Qnity, to give a quick wrap-up, will focus on chemicals specific to semis, and interconnect solutions - meaning Wifi/5G. Qnity will be correlated to semi demand and semi-industry trends. I will keep an eye on it, but I have businesses that I consider to be more interesting in this industry.

Arguments for this change and for DuPont concerns, unlocking value and effective capital deployment alongside attractive electronics positioning. I prefer to focus on the appeal of DuPont's legacy, where Tyvek and Kevlar are two technologies I view as reasons #1 and #2 for investing in DuPont while they unlock further value in other areas. Both of these legacy chemicals/technologies have attractive profit margins and have the sort of market presence and pricing power that I doubt will decline over the years. I consider it sort of an American Sika AG (OTCPK:SXYAY), a Swiss company in building sealants that I cover.

The risks, which I will cover in more detail, however, are that DuPont's spin-off does not actually result in any financial efficiencies or gain, instead resulting in operational challenges, market volatility - and we also know that any spin-off is always a process. Even the company itself acknowledges these risks.

To add further to this, DuPont still has billions in potential PFAS-related liabilities, which now have a smaller operational base to generate cash flow for coverage, so consider that as well, as it does impact value and contribute to some of the volatility we're seeing in the company.

Fundamentally, DuPont is attractive and, based on its legacy operations, certainly has a moat, albeit a small one (because it's not exactly overinvesting in new tech through high R&D). And while comparing DuPont to more basic material-chemicals businesses like BASF can only be done carefully and the company is undervalued next to its closest peers, we also need to consider the company's risks.

Highlights in Q1 include good growth and solid margin increase. It's what caused the company's relatively swift bounce up. EPS increased 30% on an adjusted basis, and the company made the plans for Qnity more concrete.

Tariffs certainly carry an impact potential for the company, but DuPont is moving to reduce it.

However, the above also illustrates the difficulty even for a huge company to do just that.

2025E guidance calls for net sales of just south of $13B for the complete company, with an operating EBITDA of up to $3.375B, and adjusted EPS of at least $4.3/share. That would mean a 5-6% EPS growth for the year.

DuPont is BBB+ rated, is a very low-leverage company, and remains a company that more often than not beats estimates, at least over the past 10 years. It's a company that I view as often being underestimated - that's one of the reasons I bought it when I did, but I also try not to overstate what's possible for the business, and this is one of the reasons I sold 50% of my position about 9 months ago. The company's fundamentals are attractive, but the yield at 2.2% is sub-par to most of the chemical companies I invest in. This results in a relatively "narrow" corridor of appeal.

My estimate forward includes Qnity as part of the company, and I have not yet decided what to do about the Qnity shares - currently, I tend towards the decision to sell them, if I still hold DD shares during the spinoff phase.

Evaluating forward potential is tricky. DuPont has underperformed the market since I last wrote about it, and most indicators tell me that the company is unlikely to outperform.

Indicators such as the ones below, which include the valuation.

Valuation for DuPont - Upside to a fair value of $90/share, but I'm more hesitant about the P/E and about growth

In my last article, I put the company's fair value at about $80/share, which, given the upside available back then, was insufficient, leading to my Hold rating. The same is unfortunately true at this time.

The company trades at below $75/share, but the trouble comes when we start estimating forward growth. On a 20-year basis, the company manages about 16-17x P/E, making it 16.5x. The company's current average weighted earnings multiple is 17.73x, which comes to a slight overvaluation. Positive arguments for the company include the EPS growth potential - about 10-11% per year in 2026E and 2027E - but to that I say that we don't know at this time how much will be Qnity (which is supposedly spun off this year) and how much legacy DuPont. Furthermore, risk factors also include PFAS litigation as well as tariffs, neither of which I believe are fully realized and included in the current estimates for double-digit growth.

The one argument we have is the company's tendency to outperform estimates, but over time, this has not helped DD investors to outperform the market. In fact, as you can see, for every single specific period, DD has underperformed the SPY, which you can also see on the Seeking Alpha tool here.

Therefore, I say that investment valuation, meaning the value at which you buy, is absolutely crucial. I would not buy DD above its 15-20 year multiple, and with the added uncertainty, I would not compromise on this one inch. I'm maintaining $80/share fair value at this time, but I put the price target ("PT") at $68/share or about 16.5x current earnings, when I see a conservative upside of 15% annualized or above, inclusive of the yield. That's the earnings-related thesis. Your current potential RoR looks as follows, based on a 20-year average.

Peers can offer us some insight and alternatives. The company's direct peers are businesses like 3M (MMM) and Xylem (XYL), both of which are currently overvalued as well. Value can instead be found in basic materials, chemicals, and/or distribution. My choice at this time, if you were to be interested, is German Brenntag SE (OTCPK:BNTGF). The company trades at sub-14%, has a 1.5% higher yield than DD, is also BBB+ rated, and works only with distribution. Operational results are set to grow by over 12% per year over the next 3 years, and at the same multiple as DD, the company has an annualized upside of over 23%.

That makes this company, in my view, a better choice here, which Is also why I invest in the business.

The risks for my thesis are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risk for my neutral rating is one of outperformance from the Qnity spinoff. Investors could see significant returns if the stocks of both companies appreciated higher than the individual values of these segments as they are today, which is also part of what I believe the company counts on. Beyond this, I view DD's end markets and operations as especially characteristic of not delivering surprises, as we can see from the company's long-term results and comparisons to the SPY.

A higher PT would therefore, to me, entail a higher likelihood for post-Qnity outperformance.

My thesis for the company is as follows.

Thesis

  • Chemicals/Industrials are still an investable segment, and I continue to invest major capital in businesses such as BASF and Brenntag, among others.
  • DD continues to combine the appeal of specialty chemicals with a great market position, excellent fundamentals, and a yield that could still increase, even if the current plan makes it more likely that growth is to come from different ways (specifically, a spin-off). My PT for the company reflects the company in its current, not-spunoff state.
  • I give DuPont a "HOLD" with a PT of $68/share from a fair value of $80/share for the longer term, legitimizing some of the company's premium that we see here, but not as much as in my previous article from 2024. I would be careful going higher, and view other companies, such as Brenntag, as materially more attractive.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansions/reversions.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $68/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of July 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Boliden AB (publ) 2025 Q2 - Results - Earnings Call Presentation
data.description: The following slide deck was published by Boliden AB (publ) in conjunction with their 2025 Q2 earnings call.

data.title: Super Micro Computer: Liquid Cooling Can Become Commoditized (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
SMCI Stock: Fiscal Q3 Recap

My last work on Super Micro Computer, Inc. (NASDAQ:SMCI) was published on May 8. That article was titled “Super Micro Computer Q3: The Less-Discussed Topics (Rating Downgrade)”. As stated in the title, the purpose of that article was to explain the following reasons that led me to downgrade the stock’s rating (to a buy rating from my earlier strong buy rating) after reviewing its Q3 earnings report (ER).

This article serves as a review of Super Micro Computer, Inc.'s fiscal Q3 2025 earnings report. It focuses on a few topics less mentioned, including its Silicon Valley expansion projects, EBITDA margin pressure, and also declining fixed asset turnover rates.

The thesis of this article is to argue for another rating downgrade to hold given the changes surrounding the stock since my last article. In the remainder of this article, I will focus on the top 2 changes that prompted this downgrade: the drastic valuation expansion and the potential for its liquid cooling technology to become commoditized.

I will start with the valuation expansion. At the time of my last publication, the stock price was $32.1, translating into a FWD P/E of 15.8x. Since then, the stock has seen a significant change of more than 64 (considerably outperforming both the S&P 500 and the overall tech sector, as shown below). This change is ahead of its earnings projections, and FWD P/E has expanded to over 25x. As to be argued next, this valuation, when combined with profitability and growth prospects, no longer justifies a buy rating in my model.

SMCI Stock: Liquid Cooling and Margin Pressure

One of SMCI’s key growth thrusts is its liquid cooling racks, and this technology certainly has an important role to play for our digital future, in my view. For example, for its next-gen liquid cooling solution, the company claims that it can help reduce data center power by ~40% vs. air-cooled installations. Even so, I expect such technology to become a commodity quickly, as many companies now offer their own versions. You can find a list of the top Liquid Cooling Companies compiled by DataCentre Magazine, and you will see that notable competitors include players dedicated to liquid racking as well as more integrated players like Dell Technologies (DELL) and Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE). Furthermore, I expect competitors like Dell and HPE to enjoy an advantage with enterprise customers.

As such, I expect these competitors to keep pressuring SMCI’s margins in the already-competitive AI server/rack market. As an example, the chart below displays SMCI's quarterly gross profit margin, and the downward trend is quite obvious in recent years. To wit, starting with a high of 18.76% in early 2023, the gross profit margin has steadily declined to its current value of 9.57%. This is not only far below the average gross profit margin of 14.94% over the past 3 years but also among the lowest levels in this period.

SMCI Stock: Valuation Risk Heightened

Despite the margin pressure and intensifying competition, SCMI’s valuation ratios have expanded significantly since my last writing, as aforementioned. As shown in the table below, its FWD P/E (on a non-GAAP basis) is now 25.36; this is slightly above the sector median (by about 4%) and vastly above its own 5-year averages of 16.33x (by more than 55%). In contrast, at the time of the last writing, the stock was trading at a slight discount to its 5-year average P/E and drastically below the sector median.

The current P/E ratio also indicates heightened valuation risks when adjusted for growth. The table below is based on consensus projections of its EPS for the next 3 years. As seen, its EPS is projected to grow from $2.08 in FY 2025 to $2.75 in FY 2026 and further to $3.49 by FY 2027, representing a compound annual growth rate (CAGR) of 18.8% for the next 3 years. At a FWD P/E of 25.3x based on the mid-point EPS estimate, the PEG (P/E growth) ratio is considerably above the ideal threshold of 1x. Even with P/E projections based on high EPS, the same concern remains.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Before closing, a few other upside and downside risks are worth mentioning. On the downside, besides the above issues, I expect the company to continue needing additional capital to fund its revenue growth. I see the company consuming cash at a good pace, judging from its latest financial statements, which indicate that it may need more capital raises to fund working capital and CAPEX projects.

As a reflection of such needs, the next chart illustrates the quarterly share count of its outstanding shares. You can see a notable increase over the past few years. The reading was around 510 million shares in 2021. It has been on an overall rising trend since then and now hovers around 596.77 million shares. Long-term debt has increased drastically too over the same period, as seen in the second chart below. These capital-raising measures can be dilutive to existing shareholders.

On the positive side, its next-gen liquid cooling solution can help reduce data center power by ~40% as aforementioned, which is a substantial saving for its customers, as modern high-performance computing uses an enormous amount of power. The power demand is only going to increase, in my view, with the expansion of AI technologies. For example, Goldman Sachs’ research suggests that AI applications can drive a 165% increase in data center power demand by 2030. Such a large increase could help expand the addressable market for SMCI racks while competing expands. In the meantime, SMCI’s manufacturing capacity is also larger compared to many competitors, offering it the advantage of economies of scale.

All told, this article was triggered mostly by two changes surrounding SMCI stock since my last article: the drastic P/E expansion and the competition intensification in liquid cooling products. From experiences, I’ve learned that successful investing requires a good understanding of both long- and near-term risks. This is why delineating these risks is the starting point for the strategies used in our Investing Group Service (Envision Early Retirement). In the case of SMCI, we now see a neural reward/risk ratio both for the near term and also long term, given the valuation risks and the potential for the commoditization of liquid cooling racks, leading to a downgrade of its rating to hold.

data.title: Clarkson: It's Perhaps A Good Time To Be Contrarian
data.description:
Introduction

Since my last article on Clarkson PLC (OTCPK:CKNHF) was published, the share price has lost approximately 15% of its value. Clarkson also mentioned it now expects the tariffs as announced by the USA will have a negative impact on its financial results, but the company will remain profitable.

Clarkson is trading with CKN on the London Stock Exchange, and with an average daily volume exceeding 100,000 shares per day, it clearly is the most liquid listing to trade in the company’s stock. As Clarkson reports its financial results in GBP, I will use the GBP as base currency throughout this article. At the current share price of approximately 33.35 GBP, the market capitalization of the company is roughly 1B GBP.

This article is meant as an update to previous coverage. Please read the older article to get a better understanding of Clarkson’s business model and its operational divisions. Clarkson’s main division is the shipbroking business, where it helps clients to buy and sell ships but also to find counterparties for charter agreements and lease agreements.

The asset-light business model still results in a strong free cash flow result

Before discussing the current situation and the trading updates, I think it makes sense to take a step back and revisit the 2024 cash flow results, as that will set the tone of what we can expect in a normalized situation (read: in a tariff-light world with relatively stable FX rates).

Clarkson reported a total revenue of approximately 661M GBP in 2024, which represents an increase of just over 3% compared to the preceding year. The COGS and administrative expenses increased at a slightly higher rate, and after also including the acquisition-related costs to the tune of 3.2M GBP in FY 2024, the reported operating profit of 98.5M GBP was approximately 1.5% lower than in 2023. Excluding these acquisition-related expenses, the operating profit would have increased by approximately 2%.

Fortunately, the company also reported a substantial increase in its net interest income as its balance sheet contains a net cash position (Clarkson’s has no financial debt; only lease liabilities and trade payables). This helped to boost the pre-tax income by approximately 3% while the net attributable income increased to 84.9M GBP, for an EPS of 277 pence. That’s an increase of just 1% compared to the FY 2023 EPS due to the higher average tax rate and the impact of a 3M GBP acquisition-related non-recurring expense. Excluding the latter, the EPS would have come in at just under 287 pence, a 4% increase compared to the 275 pence net income in FY 2023.

As I focused on Clarkson’s cash flow performance in the previous article, it only makes sense to have another look at the cash flow generated by Clarkson’s in 2024. As you can see below, the operating cash flow was almost 115M GBP on a reported basis, but this includes a 26M GBP working capital contribution, whereas it does not include the 14.9M GBP in interest and dividends received by Clarkson's, while it also doesn’t include the 12.7M GBP in lease and interest payments.

This means that on an adjusted basis, the operating cash flow was approximately 93M GBP (and this includes the allowance for bonus accruals, as Clarkson’s offers a very bonus-heavy compensation structure).

After also deducting the 7.3M GBP in investments (excluding M&A-related cash outflow), the underlying net free cash flow was approximately 90M GBP, of which roughly 88M GBP was attributable to the common shareholders of Clarkson PLC. This represents just under 290 pence.

Clarkson is proud of its dividend history, as the company has increased its dividend every single year in over 20 years' time. While the current gross yield of 3.3% isn’t spectacular, it still represents a payout ratio of less than 50% of the earnings.

At the end of 2024, Clarkson PLC has 493.5M GBP in cash (and term deposits), whereas the balance sheet does not contain any debt. However, given the bonus-heavy structure of Clarkson's, I feel the 250M GBP in accrued but unpaid bonuses should be considered a financial liability, as those will eventually have to be paid out (either in cash or through the creation of new shares, and while the latter wouldn’t result in a cash outflow, it would have a dilutive impact on the per-share performance). This means that I am using a pro forma net cash position of approximately 243M GBP for approximately 791 pence per share. This calculation excludes the 12.3M GBP pension surplus.

This means the current enterprise value of Clarkson’s is just around 750M GBP.

As part of its AGM update, Clarkson mentioned it now expects a pre-tax profit of 85-95M GBP as the weaker US Dollar also has a negative impact on Clarkson's financial results, which are reported in GBP. Applying an effective tax rate of 22.5% to the midpoint of the guidance implies a net profit of around 70M GBP, of which about 68M GBP would be attributable to the shareholders of Clarkson PLC.

Investment thesis

That implies an EPS of around 220 pence, indicating the stock is currently trading at approximately 15 times earnings. I also consider the FX volatility the main risk related to an investment in Clarkson, as the majority of the company’s revenue is generated in USD while it reports in GBP.

The underlying business (expressed in USD) remains strong (albeit a bit weaker than in the previous few years), and thanks to the very robust balance sheet, the good reputation in the sector, and an asset-light business model, Clarkson PLC is getting increasingly attractive at the current share price. Given the current FX headwinds, a multi-year investment horizon would be recommended.

I currently have no position in Clarkson, but I am keeping very close tabs.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: IBM Q2 Earnings Preview: Still A Buy, Just Not Too Much
data.description:
IBM's (NYSE:IBM) Q2 2025 results approach July 23rd. The stock has seen some welcome gains over the past year.

I covered it last September and rated it a Buy at the time. Total returns are up 30% since then. As we approach Q2 for this year, is it reasonable to take the same stance?

The short answer is yes. The long answer is to consider a smaller allocation and readiness for bumps in the road in Q2 earnings updates.

Current Snapshot

Let's focus on the key things that I believe reflect IBM's intrinsic value and its risk factors, and reiterating some of my points from last year, as well as management's takeaways when Q1 results were released.

  • Annual revenue around $62B
  • Free cash flow around $12B (adjusted for financing receivables)
  • $63.3B in debt
  • $17.6B in cash

This debt has maturities staggered across decades, with manageable maturities for the foreseeable future, either to repay or refinance. It's not something I find troublesome, even if a first look appears a bit high.

These financials derive from three main business segments:

  • Software: Automation, Hybrid Cloud, Transaction Processing, Data
  • Consulting: Strategy (for customer use of AI) and Operations (applications for the AI)
  • Infrastructure: Transaction processing platform and servers

These segments primarily serve large, commercial clients, known for using software and related products to help them run businesses, with emphasis on unlocking the power of AI for customers in these segments.

It's different from the IBM of the past, known for its line of personal computers and other hardware. Today's IBM is more software-focused and less personal, dedicated to products that help businesses run better.

Previous Developments and Talking Points

In my thesis last year, AI was still developing story and market opportunity. I expected that the company would continue to see revenue growth, supported by the strong demand for AI in commercial uses.

That proved to be the case throughout 2024, with strong revenue growth and has continued so far into 2025. Management was extremely optimistic during its Q1 2025 earnings call. CFO James Kavanaugh expressed the following:

We talked about the strategic repositioning of our portfolio and our company overall to much more software-centric, platform-centric model. And we laid out a financial investment thesis, we called it our shareholder value creation thesis, that said we would take this business from a no-growth profile to the last three years kind of low single-digit profile to an accelerating revenue growth profile of mid-single-digit-plus, and that's how we guided 2025.

Amid this positive outlook, management was confident about the state of the business and guided for free cash flow of about $13.5B.

Valuation and Outlook

My previous valuation came from the following Discounted Cash Flow calculation:

Using likely FCF at the time, these basic thoughts under-girded my calculation:

  • 10% CAGR for five years and then 5% after didn't require bold assumptions
  • We could even give a multiple down to 10 in case of future headwinds
  • Dividend policy would help ensure returns are received

I think the basic assumptions were sound and that year-over-year results show IBM essentially following along those lines to date.

If we update to include the forward FCF for 2025 and new share count, that raises the intrinsic value to $251 per share. The current market price is a bit higher, but I believe it's similar to the situation I described last time: a mostly fair value for modest growth, with potentially higher growth to be enjoyed for free.

A case can therefore still be made that IBM is a Buy today, but as the risk-reward asymmetry isn't quite as favorable, we need to consider the matters in Q2 that can confirm or worsen the outlook:

  • Lost business from government cuts
  • Impact of tariffs and trade deals
  • How segments will be affected

Again, I want to emphasize that I think Q2 will serve to confirm or worsen the outlook. While management did praise the Trump Administration (in the Q1 earnings call) for an agenda it believes will help the economy, they did discuss the near-term uncertainty for their own business:

In the near-term, uncertainty may cause clients to pause and take a wait and see approach. However, the value of hybrid cloud automation, data sovereignty, and on-premise solutions becomes even more critical in volatile windows. Recent conversations that I've had with clients reflect this view of the current environment. These conversations vary by industry, business, and geography.

While this earnings call occurred shortly after Liberation Day and the turmoil in capital markets for the month of April, recovery of asset values doesn't mean that major businesses (and thus IBM's customers) believe they have everything figured out. In fact, for many businesses, not just IBM, Q2 earnings calls are going to tell us a lot more about how the tariffs, even just the threat of them, impacted their business models and priorities going forward.

For IBM, this translates to how customers choose their capex, in terms of size and where it's spent. Management did hint which segment would likely be hit first:

Consulting is also more susceptible to discretionary pullbacks and DOGE-related initiatives.

In essence, customers can use IBM's core products without also using the consulting service, but it doesn't work the other way. As this is the lowest-margin business, it would be the least damaging to FCF growth. What we will need to gauge is if the other segments' growth can continue, thereby offsetting it.

If, however, hesitation by customers is more major, leading to delays in development of AI assets because the extent of their need is perceived as lower, this would impact the other segments and FCF growth more heavily.

Conclusion

When I wrote about IBM last year, I talked about managing one's risk, should they invest in this business, by having an exit plan. I think an extra step is to take a smaller position than one would have last time, given these risk factors and the less favorable valuation. IBM is on the other side of a recent turnaround and is worth a modest allocation as a bet on AI. A concentrated bet only makes sense with a lower entry price, barring any Q2 disasters.

As long as one sizes appropriately, however, I think IBM has a place in a long-term portfolio.
